perturbation.original_text: Employee shall not, directly or indirectly, use any Confidential Information for any purpose other than the benefit of a Banking Organization, and shall not directly or indirectly, disclose, communicate, deliver, exhibit or provide any Confidential Information to any Person, except other Employees or Agents of a Banking Organization who have a need to know such Confidential Information for a proper corporate or business purpose, as required in the normal course of Employee’s service as an employee. Nothing in this Section 4 , or any other provision of this Agreement, shall be construed to prohibit Employee from reporting conduct to, providing truthful information to, or participating in any investigation or proceeding brought or conducted by any federal or state government agency or self-regulatory organization.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee shall not, directly or indirectly, use any Confidential Information for any purpose other than the benefit of a Banking Organization, and shall not directly or indirectly, disclose, communicate, deliver, exhibit or provide any Confidential Information to any Person, except other Employees or Agents of a Banking Organization who have a need to know such Confidential Information for a proper corporate or business purpose, as required in the normal course of Employee’s service as an employee. Nothing in this Section 4, or any other provision of this Agreement, shall be construed to allow Employee to report conduct to, provide truthful information to, or participate in any investigation or proceeding brought or conducted by any federal or state government agency or self-regulatory organization without express written consent of the Banking Organization.
perturbation.explanation: This change restricts the employee from reporting illegal conduct to government agencies without the employer's permission, which violates whistleblower protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1513 - Retaliating against a witness, victim, or informant
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text directly contradicts 18 U.S.C. § 1513, which protects individuals who provide information to law enforcement or government agencies from retaliation. By requiring employer consent before reporting conduct, the agreement creates a chilling effect and obstructs justice.

Retaliating against a witness, victim, or an informant (a)(1) Whoever kills or attempts to kill another person with intent to retaliate against any person for- (A) the attendance of a witness or party at an official proceeding, or any testimony given or any record, document, or other object produced by a witness in an official proceeding; or (B) providing to a law enforcement off
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a requirement for express written consent from the Banking Organization before an employee can report conduct to government agencies or participate in investigations. This directly contradicts the intent of whistleblower protection laws, specifically 18 U.S.C. § 1513, which aims to prevent retaliation against individuals providing information to law enforcement. The original text explicitly allows such reporting without restrictions. The modified text undermines the protection offered to informants, making them vulnerable to employer control and potential retaliation, which obstructs justice. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 18 USC 1513 concerning retaliation against informants, and while it doesn't explicitly prohibit requiring consent, the change creates a chilling effect that discourages lawful reporting.

perturbation.original_text: Therefore, in consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of one (1) year immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution (“Restriction Period”), for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer; or b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer.
perturbation.changed_text: Therefore, in consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of five (5) years immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution (“Restriction Period”), for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer; or b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Indiana law, which requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration.
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana Code 24-1-71 regarding trade secrets, while not directly addressing non-compete duration, implies reasonableness. A five-year non-solicitation clause is likely an unreasonable restraint on trade under Indiana common law principles of non-compete enforceability, particularly if the employee's role doesn't involve highly sensitive trade secrets or confidential information. Indiana courts assess the reasonableness of non-compete clauses based on factors like the nature of the business, the scope of the restriction, and the hardship on the employee.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Indiana General Assembly You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from one year to five years. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this may be unreasonable under Indiana law. The Law Explanation indicates that a five-year non-solicitation clause might be an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially if the employee doesn't handle highly sensitive information. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is incomplete, the Law Explanation stating that Indiana courts assess the reasonableness of non-compete clauses based on factors such as the nature of the business, scope of restriction and hardship on the employee supports the conclusion that extending the restriction period to five years could be deemed unreasonable and therefore unenforceable, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of two (2) years immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: c. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any Employee to terminate his or her employment with a Banking Organization, or offer or provide employment (whether such employment is with Employee or any other Person) either on a full-time or part-time, consulting or Independent Contractor basis; or d. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any Independent Contractors or Agents to terminate their contract or relationship with a Banking Organization or discontinue providing services to a Banking Organization and/or Customers; or e. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any suppliers, vendors or others who were supplying services or goods to a Banking Organization during the one year period prior to Employee’s termination, to terminate, reduce, limit or change their business or relationship with a Banking Organization.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of five (5) years immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: c. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any Employee to terminate his or her employment with a Banking Organization, or offer or provide employment (whether such employment is with Employee or any other Person) either on a full-time or part-time, consulting or Independent Contractor basis; or d. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any Independent Contractors or Agents to terminate their contract or relationship with a Banking Organization or discontinue providing services to a Banking Organization and/or Customers; or e. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any suppliers, vendors or others who were supplying services or goods to a Banking Organization during the one year period prior to Employee’s termination, to terminate, reduce, limit or change their business or relationship with a Banking Organization.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the employee non-solicitation period to five years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Indiana law, which requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration.
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana Code 24-1-71 regarding trade secrets, while not directly addressing non-compete duration, implies reasonableness. A five-year employee non-solicitation clause is likely an unreasonable restraint on trade under Indiana common law principles of non-compete enforceability, particularly if the employee's role doesn't involve highly sensitive trade secrets or confidential information. Indiana courts assess the reasonableness of non-compete clauses based on factors like the nature of the business, the scope of the restriction, and the hardship on the employee.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from two to five years. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that a five-year non-solicitation period may be unreasonable under Indiana law. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that such a clause could be an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially if the employee's role doesn't involve sensitive information. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' as the changed text sets a requirement (five-year non-solicitation period) that potentially violates state law regarding reasonable restraint of trade, making it potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of Employee’s employment, Employee shall immediately return to the Bank (if subsequent to the Effective Time) or Heritage (if prior to the Effective Time), all Banking Organizations documents and property, including, but not limited to, Confidential Information, computer discs, manuals, reports, files, memoranda, records, door and file keys, passwords and access codes, and any other physical or tangible things that Employee received, prepared, or helped prepare in connection with the Employee’s employment, and Employee shall not retain any copies, duplicates, reproductions or excerpts thereof. At the Bank’s or Heritage’s request, Employee shall provide written verification under the penalties for perjury of his/her compliance with this covenant.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of Employee’s employment, Employee shall immediately return to the Bank (if subsequent to the Effective Time) or Heritage (if prior to the Effective Time), all Banking Organizations documents and property, including, but not limited to, Confidential Information, computer discs, manuals, reports, files, memoranda, records, door and file keys, passwords and access codes, and any other physical or tangible things that Employee received, prepared, or helped prepare in connection with the Employee’s employment, and Employee shall not retain any copies, duplicates, reproductions or excerpts thereof. Employee is not allowed to copy or save contact information of clients and/or employees or verification of this compliance.
perturbation.explanation: The change states that Employee is not allowed to copy or save contact information of clients and/or employees. This could conflict with the National Labor Relations Act if it is interpreted to restrict the employee's ability to retain contact information necessary for future organizing activities.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 7 of the NLRA grants employees the right to self-organize, form, join, or assist labor organizations. Preventing an employee from retaining contact information of clients and/or employees potentially hinders their ability to communicate with and organize fellow employees, thereby infringing on their rights under the NLRA.

perturbation.justification: The changed text prevents employees from retaining contact information, which the Law Explanation argues could hinder their ability to organize, infringing on rights under the NLRA. The change directly restricts access to information that could be used for union activities, potentially conflicting with the rights granted by the NLRA to form or assist labor organizations. Although the scraped snippets are limited, the Law Explanation explicitly mentions Section 7 of the NLRA regarding the right to self-organize, and the changed text appears to directly impede this right, creating a legal contradiction as it potentially violates federal labor law.
